message:
fix(fcp-e2e/telegram): align allow-path tokens with capability verifier (br-1maun)

Root-cause for telegram_allow_valid_token_connector_suite_passes and
telegram_send_message_returns_message_id failing on main: the test's
build_token helper was constructing tokens whose claim shape no
longer matches what the current capability verifier expects.

Two separate defects, both surfaced by the 8n0rm.6 / C3.4 tightening:

  1. Capability-vs-operation confusion. The test was passing the
     operation id (`telegram.send_message`) as the capability id
     argument. But the connector's introspection declares that
     `telegram.send_message` requires capability `telegram.send` (a
     permission *class*, not the op id — same pattern as
     discord.send / discord.send_message, gcp.read / gcp.get, etc.).
     After 8n0rm.6 removed the legacy OPERATIONS fallback from the
     verifier, the mismatch surfaces at capability.rs:2001 where
     `g.capability != required_capability` returns false for every
     grant — so the verifier emits OperationNotGranted (FCP-3003).
     Fixed by passing `"telegram.send"` as the capability arg and
     leaving the operations list as `["telegram.send_message"]` so
     synthesize_grants_from_legacy_operations produces the correct
     {capability: "telegram.send", operation: "telegram.send_message"}
     grant.

  2. Missing constraints claim. Capability constraints are now
     mandatory per C3.4 default-deny (capability.rs:2017-2029). A
     token with no `constraints` claim is rejected with FCP-3001
     "token has no constraints — null constraints are rejected
     (C3.4)". The build_token helper wasn't setting any. Fixed by
     attaching a `CapabilityConstraints { resource_allow: ["*"], .. }`
     via `constraints_cbor` — wildcard `"*"` is the documented
     catch-all (fcp-core/capability.rs:1320).

Also fixed test 4 (telegram_send_message_returns_message_id) which
had the same capability/operation confusion in its direct-connector
path. Test 1 (default_deny) is intentionally left alone — its
intent is to grant `telegram.get_file` and invoke `telegram.send_message`,
expecting denial. The denial still happens (neither capability
matches), so the test's observable behavior is unchanged.

Added ciborium to fcp-e2e dev-dependencies so the test can serialize
constraints to CBOR for `constraints_cbor`.

Did NOT weaken either assertion. The assertion at line 422
(`assert!(report.passed)`) is now satisfied because the invoke
actually succeeds end-to-end, not because the check was relaxed.

Verified with
  CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/fcp-review-A cargo test \
    -p fcp-e2e --no-default-features --features telegram \
    --test telegram_compliance_e2e
→ 4 passed, 0 failed.

Closes br-1maun. Unblocks br-7e15h (mock-hit assertion coverage gap).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
